phrasalVerbs.definition: To practise and improve your skills or knowledge of something.
examples: I need to brush up on my Spanish before the trip.
She's brushing up on her maths for the exam.
You should brush up on your interview skills.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To continue to do something; to move at the same speed as someone or something.
examples: Keep up the good work!
I can't keep up with all the new technology.
She walks so fast that I can barely keep up.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To work at different times from usual because you have not worked enough at the normal times.
examples: I need to make up the hours I missed yesterday.
Can I make up the class next week?
She's making up for lost time by working weekends.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To improve; to learn a new skill or start a habit without intending to.
examples: Business is starting to pick up after the slow season.
She picked up French while living in Paris.
I picked up some bad habits at my last job.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To get information on a particular subject by reading a lot about it.
examples: I need to read up on the topic before the meeting.
She read up about the company before her interview.
You should read up on local customs before travelling.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To have a particular amount of time away from work.
examples: I'm taking a week off to visit my family.
She took the afternoon off to go to the dentist.
He rarely takes time off work.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To accept some work or responsibility.
examples: She took on extra responsibilities at work.
I can't take on any more projects right now.
He took on the role of team leader.

fillInExercises.sentence: I need to ___ ___ on my German before the business trip.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Brush up on' means to practise and improve your existing skills.

fillInExercises.sentence: It's hard to ___ up with all the changes in technology.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Keep up' means to stay at the same level or pace as something.

fillInExercises.sentence: I missed two days of work, so I have to ___ ___ the time.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Make up' means to work extra time to compensate for missed hours.

fillInExercises.sentence: Sales have started to ___ up after a slow quarter.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Pick up' means to improve or increase.

fillInExercises.sentence: Before the interview, I ___ ___ on the company's history.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Read up on' means to research a topic by reading about it.

fillInExercises.sentence: I'm ___ a few days off next month for a holiday.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Take off' means to have time away from work.

fillInExercises.sentence: She ___ ___ more work than she could handle.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Take on' means to accept work or responsibility.

fillInExercises.sentence: He ___ up photography as a hobby last year.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Pick up' can mean to learn a new skill, often informally.

lines.speaker: Sophie
lines.text: How's the new job going? Are you [keeping up|keep up] with everything?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: 'Keep up' means to stay at the same level or pace as something.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: *keep up* — To continue to do something; to move at the same speed as someone or something.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: How's the new job going? Are you keeping up with everything?
explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not confuse this with other items from the lesson — here you need *keep up* (*keep up*).

wrongOptions.brush up (on): *brush up (on)* (To practise and improve your skills or knowledge of something) does not match the meaning needed in this line.
wrongOptions.make up: *make up* (To work at different times from usual because you have not worked enough at the normal times) does not match the meaning needed in this line.
wrongOptions.pick up: *pick up* (To improve) does not match the meaning needed in this line.
wrongOptions.read up (on/about): *read up (on/about)* (To get information on a particular subject by reading a lot about it) does not match the meaning needed in this line.
wrongOptions.take off: *take off* (To have a particular amount of time away from work) does not match the meaning needed in this line.
wrongOptions.take on: *take on* (To accept some work or responsibility) does not match the meaning needed in this line.

explanationContent.usefulTip: In conversation, *keep up* is often used in informal spoken English.
